ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Japanese curry rice; FOODON_00002809 (curry dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (rice-based dish) used as closest defensible match; no dedicated Japanese curry rice code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for Japanese curry rice.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Japanese curry rice is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 150 g of steamed white rice topped with 150 g of curry stew containing meat and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Japanese Curry Rice — Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are preserved as supplied. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 1.5 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for beef and vegetable curry with rice (USDA SR Legacy #22904) as a secondary cross-reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Japanese curry rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on the analogous entry for white rice with mixed curry sauce (GI ~60–65 range) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (60) × available carbohydrate per serving (25.19 g/100g × 300 g serving = 75.57 g) / 100 = 45.3 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 693 mg sodium, representing 30% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request low-sodium curry roux, avoid adding soy sauce or salt, and limit portion size to one serving to keep sodium intake within DASH diet targets; the potassium content (327 mg/100g) partially offsets sodium load and supports blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load (25.19 g/100g; ~75.6 g per 300 g serving) from white rice with a medium GI of approximately 60, partially mitigated by 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, substitute brown rice to lower the glycaemic index, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption and reduce postprandial glycaemic excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 1.5 g/100g and sodium at 231 mg/100g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; cholesterol is reported as 0 mg/100g by HPB.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should choose chicken over red meat to reduce saturated fat intake, select low-sodium roux, and keep serving size to 200–250 g to limit cumulative sodium and saturated fat exposure per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Curry spices (turmeric, cumin, coriander) provide anti-inflammatory curcumin; however, potato is a nightshade vegetable and red meat increases arachidonic acid load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer chicken over beef or lamb, request potato-free curry, and benefit from the anti-inflammatory polyphenols in turmeric and cumin present in the curry spice blend.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese Curry Rice (カレーライス, Karē Raisu; Chinese: 日式咖喱饭) is a popular Japanese comfort dish consisting of steamed white rice served alongside or topped with a thick, mildly spiced curry stew. The stew is typically made from a proprietary Japanese curry roux block — a blend of curry powder, flour, fat, and seasonings — simmered with diced onion, carrot, potato, and a protein such as chicken, beef, or pork. Unlike South Asian curries, Japanese curry is sweeter, milder, and thicker in consistency, with a glossy, sauce-like texture that coats the rice. The dish is widely consumed across Japan and has become a staple in Singaporean Japanese restaurants and food courts. Per 100 g, it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with a notable dietary fibre content of 3.7 g from the vegetable components. Sodium is 231 mg/100g, which is moderate but accumulates significantly in a full 300 g serving. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 60 (medium) due to the white rice base, though fibre from vegetables moderates postprandial glucose response. Clinically, portion control is essential for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. Substituting brown rice and using low-sodium roux are the two most impactful modifications for health-conscious consumers. The dish is not suitable for gluten-free diets as curry roux blocks contain wheat flour.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous entry and clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from an approved peer-reviewed source (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) and is documented as such.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5 g/100g) is not reported in the HPB source for this entry; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef and vegetable curry with rice as the closest approved-source analogue. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Japanese curry rice preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the HPB source. 4. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on white rice with mixed curry sauce from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for Japanese curry rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Input record contained a typographical error in the food name ('Japanes Curry Rice'); corrected to 'Japanese Curry Rice' in food_name; original spelling preserved in aliases for retrieval continuity. 6. The rheumatoid_arthritis health suitability entry from the input record has been migrated to additional_conditions with concern_level set to moderate; the original input marked suitable=false but the reasoning cited anti-inflammatory spice benefits alongside pro-inflammatory risks, warranting a moderate rather than binary classification.
